And even today, the progress – and even the achievements already achieved – is not guaranteed. About 5 million women live in Israel – slightly more than half the population. text: The birth rate in Israel stands at almost 3 children per woman – almost double the average in OECD countries. But behind the impressive data, deep gaps are also hidden. In the labor market, women earn an average of only 68% of men's wages. After the birth of the first child, the so-called "maternal fine" occurs: a 30% decrease in income that can last for almost a decade. In Arab society there is also sometimes a "marriage fine", a sharp decline in wages as early as the first year of marriage. Even when women get an education in desirable areas, the road to equality is still long. The proportion of STEM-educated students has doubled in the past decade, reaching about 40%, but the proportion of women in the high-tech industry has hardly changed.

text: 2 million Israelis are the product of an active social structure, of networking and of repeated choice not to be left alone. In recent days, the threat of Iranian missiles has dictated the order of our day, and the falling arenas are charging heavy prices in life and property. Already at the beginning of the current event it can be noted as a fact that despite two years plus of a grinding war, the reservists continue to stabilize and the public continues to follow instructions. With great respect to all security forces engaged in the work of destroying the Ayatollah regime in Iran, the existence of the State of Israel is based on the State of Israel's community resilience. But in order to understand the secret of the power of the home front, you may need to get away from here.

title: כדי להבין את סוד עוצמת העורף צריך להרחיק
text: After the severe earthquakes in Japan, researchers sought to understand why some communities recovered relatively quickly, while others collapsed or dispersed. The surprising finding was that neither the socio-economic level nor the scope of government investment determined the future of the community. The most significant factor was the density of the community's social networks. Professor Daniel Adlerich showed that a community survives because of three simple circles of connection. The first is the inner connectionthe people who know each other, who call, who notice when someone is gone. The second is the link between communities – the ability to share information, equipment, and support across neighborhood or city boundaries.

title: כדי להבין את סוד עוצמת העורף צריך להרחיק
text: And the third is the connection to state institutions – an open, fast and direct channel between the needs on the ground and the decision makers. It sounds like a theory. But in Israel of 2025 it takes place every day. A neighbor who makes sure the old man from the second floor goes down to the shelter in time. A group of parents who arrange transportation for children of reserve families. A municipality that transfers emergency equipment to a nearby settlement that has been damaged. A head of authority who speaks directly to residents rather than through a protocol document. These daily operations accumulate into the infrastructure of resilience.

title: כדי להבין את סוד עוצמת העורף צריך להרחיק
text: The ability to be there for each other is not just a point, but together The ability to be there for each other joins a crystallizing social network that creates a layer in the community's resilience of the State of Israel. A bridged social network is a social network between parallel communities that know how to share resources and information with each other, our ability to help the communities affected by the missiles is what will determine the degree of ability to recover and perhaps in the future even thrive again. Community resilience is the human capital, which largely determines, especially against the given existential challenges of the State of Israel, whether the State of Israel will prosper or retreat.

title: כדי להבין את סוד עוצמת העורף צריך להרחיק
text: At the individual level, each and every one of us is required to ask: Where am I within this network, what are the responsibilities and locality during the current attack. At the general level there is a responsibility for the authorities to assist each other and government officials to be able to communicate with the community directly. Of course, the existence of the State of Israel rests on military might and deterrence, but the State of Israel's ability to thrive over time also depends on how connected the State of Israel's residents are to each other. We have a heavy responsibility at this time: to maintain physical security, and to consciously maintain the human infrastructure that allows us to endure. Israeli resilience is not just a happy given.

title: כדי להבין את סוד עוצמת העורף צריך להרחיק
text: Israeli resilience is an ongoing mission of each and every one of us. The writer is the president and founder of Kedma - Youth Settlement.

title: ירדן בדילמה: בין בלימת "הסהר השיעי" לחשש מהמחר
text: King Abdullah II of Jordan was the first to coined the term "Shiite Crescent" in 2004 to warn of the spread of Iranian influence in the Middle East. Over the years, King Abdullah II of Jordan has encouraged U.S. presidents to view Iran as an enemy due to Iran's subversive activities and Iran's support for terrorism. King Abdullah II of Jordan's stance toward the 2015 nuclear deal was reluctant, although King Abdullah II of Jordan avoided public criticism of President Obama. With the outbreak of Operation Harry's Roar, Jordan found Jordan in the Iranian line of fire, with local security officials reporting falls of over a hundred bodies or interception fragments in Jordan and property damage.

title: ירדן בדילמה: בין בלימת "הסהר השיעי" לחשש מהמחר
text: While in previous rounds of fighting Iranian missiles and drones were intercepted over Jordan on Iranian missiles and drones's way to Israel, this time some of the attacks were directly aimed at the territory of Jordan and carried out with the involvement of pro-Iranian militias operating from Iraq. Among the targets attacked were Al-Royshed and Mufq Al-Salti air bases in the north of Jordan, where U.S. military aircraft were placed under the Security Cooperation Agreement signed in 2021 between the two countries. Jordan also reported foiling an Iranian cyberattack designed to damage Jordan's strategic wheat reserves by disrupting storage temperature.

title: ירדן בדילמה: בין בלימת "הסהר השיעי" לחשש מהמחר
text: The Jordanian media referred to an Iranian cyberattack designed to damage the kingdom's strategic wheat reserves by disrupting storage temperature harshly as "an attempt to undermine Jordan's nutritional security and social stability". Despite Tehran's's hostile steps, Amman's's position remained ambivalent: Amman's condemned the Iranian attacks against Amman's and against the Arab Gulf states and expressed Amman's's protest to the interim official at the Iranian embassy in Amman's; at the same time, Amman's was careful to point out that Amman's was not a party to the conflict and refrained from calling for the overthrow of the Iranian regime.

title: ירדן בדילמה: בין בלימת "הסהר השיעי" לחשש מהמחר
text: In talks held by King Abdullah with world leaders, led by President Trump, King Abdullah stressed the need for dialogue and diplomacy in order to stop the escalation, resolve the crisis, and achieve calm. Considerations for Jordan's's restrained position first, internal public opinion. the Iranian regime has been dealing from the beginning with a blackout campaign on social media accusing the Iranian regime of collaborating with the "Israeli-American enemy", and in response the Iranian regime is careful to emphasize that the interception of Iranian missiles is intended to protect the sovereignty of Jordan's and the security of Jordan's's citizens and not to serve foreign interests.

title: ירדן בדילמה: בין בלימת "הסהר השיעי" לחשש מהמחר
text: This position helps the Iranian regime align with public opinion: According to a September poll, 76.2% of Jordanians see Israel as the main threat to Jordan's, while only 9% in Tehran's. This gap illustrates the differences between the perception of the threats of the palace and that of the street and the difficulty of bridging the differences between the perception of the threats of the palace and that of the street. A second consideration that explains Amman's position concerns the damage of the war. Jordan fears both direct Iranian attacks on targets in Jordan and a protracted war that will destabilize the region and the world.

title: ירדן בדילמה: בין בלימת "הסהר השיעי" לחשש מהמחר
text: As the campaign goes on, the risks of a deep and lasting blow to the Jordanian economy will increase: disrupting the flow of natural gas from Israel – which is responsible for most of the electricity produced in Jordan – will require finding more expensive alternatives; rising oil and fuel prices will deepen inflation and poverty; damaging tourism, which has only recently begun to recover from the damage caused by the war in Gaza, will cause serious damage to a major source of employment and foreign currency revenues. Third, there is skepticism in Jordan about the campaign's ability to achieve the campaign's main goal - to create the conditions necessary to change the regime in Iran.

title: ירדן בדילמה: בין בלימת "הסהר השיעי" לחשש מהמחר
text: Jordanian commentators believe that the political establishment in Iran is determined to fight for the continuation of the political establishment in Iran's rule with a cohesive and loyal ideological-religious support base, while the United States and Israel have yet to present an effective plan of action beyond the rebellion of the Iranian people. Under these circumstances, an explicit Jordanian stand against Iran could prove to be a dangerous gamble. Moreover, even in the scenario of overthrowing the regime in Iran, Jordan does not see a horizon for a new and secure order in Iran, but rather the possibility of sliding into anarchy, civil war, and power struggles – scenarios that will increase instability in the region.

title: ירדן בדילמה: בין בלימת "הסהר השיעי" לחשש מהמחר
text: Finally, Jordanian ambivalence is also associated with fear of Israeli hegemony. Despite Jordan's fundamental dislike of Iran and Jordan's commitment to the strategic alliance with the United States and peace with Israel, Jordan is also looking anxiously westward. In the eyes of many of the Jordanians, a victorious, arrogant, and unrestrained Israel could shape the Middle East without regard to a victorious, arrogant, and unrestrained Israel's eastern neighbor. The agendas promoted in Israel – including annexation in Judea and Samaria, encouraging Palestinian immigration and the erosion of the status quo in Jerusalem – are considered a grave and even existential threat to Jordan.

title: ירדן בדילמה: בין בלימת "הסהר השיעי" לחשש מהמחר
text: Therefore, an Israeli victory over Iran is seen as a conversion of one danger to another rather than an introduction to the long-awaited peace. Still, the reality in a dynamic war: Iran's aggression against Jordan spurs Jordan to reexamine Jordan's neutral stance. In addition, if it turns out that the regime in Tehran has been undermined to the ground and that there is a realistic feasibility of replacing the regime in Tehran without devolving into regional chaos, the Jordanian calculation may also change.

title: ירדן בדילמה: בין בלימת "הסהר השיעי" לחשש מהמחר
text: Two decades after Abdullah coined the concept of the Shi'ite crescent, Abdullah faced a new question: not how to contain the Shi'ite crescent's spread – but what regional order would be established in the Shi'ite crescent's place, and whether it would be more stable and secure for Abdullah's kingdom. text: The plays seen in recent days on the streets of Europe, the United States and even on the faltering social networks within Iran may seem surreal to the average Israeli viewer. To understand this, order must be made in the historical memory of the Persian people. The connection between the Persian people and the Jews is not a matter of momentary political interest – The connection between the Iranians and the Jews is an alliance that goes back more than 2,500 years.

title: "ביבי, תכה בהם": הזעקה המפתיעה שמרעידה את רחובות איראן
text: In Persian consciousness, Cyrus the Great is not only a historical figure, but a symbol of freedom and justice, who gave the Jews the right to re-establish the Temple and adhere to the Jews's faith as Jews. Today, the historical wheel is turning: the Persian people see the leader of Israel as the one who repays the Persian people "a favor for a favor." If Cyrus the Great saved the people of Israel from exile, the leader of Israel was perceived as someone who might redeem the Persian people from the internal occupation of the Ayatollah regime. For the Persian people, an Israeli attack is not a declaration of war on Iran, but an act of liberation from Islamic occupation. Had Israel refrained from action, the ancient covenant would have been fatally damaged.

text: For the Gulf states, the war with Iran is a watershed. For the first time since independence, the Gulf states have been directly, systematically and publicly attacked by Iranian missiles and drones. the Gulf states's attempt to stay out of the battle collapsed before the Gulf states's eyes. The new reality no longer allows for comfortable neutrality; The new reality imposes choice, or at least rethinking, of security perceptions. It is precisely within this crisis that there is a strategic opportunity for Israel. If Israel acts wisely, discreetly, Israel can leverage Israel's neighbors' hour of distress into a long-term move that will change the map of regional relations.

title: איך שדולת איפא"ק היממה את וושינגטון?
text: The American Israel Public Affairs Committee (AIPAC) is a lobbying organization operating in Washington, D.C. to promote U.S.-Israel relations. The American Israel Public Affairs Committee (AIPAC) was established in 1951 by lobbyist and former diplomat C. Kennan, with the aim of assisting in the transfer of the first U.S. foreign aid to Israel in 1952. Since then, The American Israel Public Affairs Committee (AIPAC) has operated as a grassroots lobby of about 6 million registered Americans (according to lobby data) seeking to strengthen the alliance between the two countries through political and public activism in the American arena. On March 3, the primaries season was opened for the November midterm elections. The first three states to set off are North Carolina, Texas and Arkansas.

title: איך שדולת איפא"ק היממה את וושינגטון?
text: Supposedly, this is only the first stop in a long political race, but March 3 provided a particularly interesting glimpse into the situation of one of the most sensitive issues in American politics today: U.S.-Israel relations. For the past year and a half, Israel's position in the American political arena has seemed to have eroded dramatically. To a large extent one of the most sensitive issues in American politics today: U.S.-Israel relations has become a politically charged and sometimes even "poisoned" issue. text: On the other hand, in recent years there has also been another phenomenon: anti-Israel rhetoric from parts of the American populist right, sometimes referred to as the right hook. In the digital space, there is sometimes a surprising connection between progressive audiences and right-wing populist audiences (also known as the "sort theory" where ideological ends are closest to an ideological meeting just like in a horseshoe). Despite the ideological gaps between progressive audiences and right-wing populist audiences, progressive audiences and right-wing populist audiences sometimes produce an online discourse focused on resistance to Israel. Significant erosion of the influence of an online discourse focused on resistance to Israel is also reflected in the polls.

title: איך שדולת איפא"ק היממה את וושינגטון?
text: According to a recent Gallup poll, gaps between political camps in the United States in relation to Israel are increasing. Among Republicans support for Israel remains very high, but among Democrats there has been significant erosion. Among young Americans, gaps between political camps in the United States in relation to Israel are even more pronounced. Against this background, in the past year, there has been a feeling that Israel has lost part of Israel's position in American politics. Some even claimed that AIPAC AIPAC itself had lost AIPAC itself's power. However, this debate sometimes ignores the way the lobby has operated for decades and the strategic change the lobby has undergone in recent years.

title: איך שדולת איפא"ק היממה את וושינגטון?
text: Since the 1970s, the lobby has developed a unique political methodology: the lobby has refrained from transferring funds directly to candidates and instead granted candidates a kind of political "certificate of kashrut". the lobby's mere support has served as a clear signal to a wide network of American donors that the lobby is a candidate who attaches importance to U.S.-Israel relations, thus indirectly providing financial support. a unique political methodology allowed lobbying to influence the political system without being exposed to old anti-Semitic claims that "Jewish money" runs American politics, while at the same time preserving support for Israel as a broad bipartisan issue. In recent years, however, the political environment in Washington has changed dramatically.

title: איך שדולת איפא"ק היממה את וושינגטון?
text: The sharp increase in campaign costs, competition with other lobbying organizations that have already operated political funds and the growing polarization between parties has led the the lobby leadership to conclude that the old rules are no longer sufficient. In late 2021 and early 2022, the lobby made a strategic decision to This transition direction and openly enter the election arena for the first time through political foundations that directly support candidates who believe in strengthening U.S.-Israel relations. This transition also included the use of the main tool of American politics in the present era, the Super PAC a mechanism that allows for almost unlimited fundraising and the referral of them to political activity.

title: איך שדולת איפא"ק היממה את וושינגטון?
text: This tool became a key component of the American electoral system following the 2010 Supreme Court ruling in Citizens United v. The Federal Election Commission has determined that certain restrictions on political spending of organizations violate the freedom of expression enshrined in the First Amendment. A 100-win-zero-loss balance sheet This new strategy has been put to the test in the election campaigns since 2022. The most notable result was in the 2024 election, when AIPAC supported 100 candidates in various races across the United States, and all of 100 candidates won. A score of 100 against zero losses made AIPAC one of the most prominent players in the American primaries.

text: And in a sharp transition to the first day of the American primaries, the day before yesterday, providing a glimpse into how This new strategy actually works. A total of 56 seats were to be selected in different races in the three states where the American primaries were opened. AIPAC's support policy for the American primaries is based on some clear rules of thumb. AIPAC does not enter any race. title: הפרדה באקדמיה היא לא הדרה – היא המפתח לשוויון
text: These days, when on the Knesset table the amendment to MK Limor Sun Har-Kelech's Student Rights Law is placed, the voices warning of "extremity" are heard again and claim that separation in school will necessarily lead to a demand for separation in the employment market. But the truth is the opposite: it is precisely the lack of possibility of advanced studies in a adapted environment and the placement of ideological barriers in the opening of faculties that are the main engine of exclusion and violation of the freedom of occupation of a large public.

title: הפרדה באקדמיה היא לא הדרה – היא המפתח לשוויון

The claim that "if they learn in separation, they will also demand separation in employment" misses the essential difference between the spaces. The workplace is usually a hierarchical and matter-of-fact space. The relationship between colleagues is based on a commitment to a professional outcome, clear job definitions, and a bitter relationship.

In contrast, academic learning in essence requires a different dynamic. academic learning is a day-to-day, continuous framework, characterized by open discourse and sharing of intellectual experiences.

title: למה איראן מתעקשת על עומאן?
text: As the world looks to Istanbul as a potential host for the dramatic talks between Tehran and Washington, behind the scenes there is a struggle over the identity of the table around which the parties will sit. While the major Sunni countries and the West are pushing for a multilateral format in Turkey, Tehran is sending a clear signal: Tehran wants the quiet sultanate of Oman. The Iranian insistence on the quiet sultanate of Oman is not accidental, and The Iranian insistence on Oman rests on a strategic and religious asset that no other country in the region has: the Ibadi stream.

title: למה איראן מתעקשת על עומאן?
text: the quiet sultanate of Oman is neither Sunni nor Shi'ite, the quiet sultanate of Oman holds a separate, ancient and moderate Islamic current, giving the quiet sultanate of Oman the status of "Switzerland of the Middle East". In a space where every diplomatic meeting is immediately painted in the colors of a religious war between Tehran's Shi'ite axis and Saudi Arabia's's Sunni axis, the quiet sultanate of Oman is the only sterile space. For Tehran, the quiet sultanate of Oman is not just a mediator – the quiet sultanate of Oman is a fair mediator, one that does not reach the table with the residues of the past or aspirations of religious hegemony. Why does Tehran reject the Turkish or Saudi embrace? The answer lies in the geopolitical tension.

title: למה איראן מתעקשת על עומאן?
text: Erdogan's Turkey aspires to regional leadership based on Sunni political Islam, and Saudi Arabia's is seen as dancing according to the Western flute. Tehran fears that at a multilateral conference in Turkey, Tehran will fall into a "sonic trap" in which neighbouring interests will set the tone. Oman, on the other hand, holds a long-standing tradition of silent diplomacy. The Ibadiya educates tolerance and conflict prevention, and this is precisely the "ladder" that Iran seeks to get off the tree of conflict. The Ibadiya was there in 2015 to pave the way for the original nuclear agreement, and The Ibadiya is there now as the U.S. carrier sails a spitting distance off Oman's shores.

title: למה איראן מתעקשת על עומאן?
text: For Supreme Leader Khamenei, Mescat is the place where one can speak "business" without feeling ideologically threatened. The choice in Oman also reflects the Iranian plight. Iran needs an intermediary that will not steal the show, but will provide the logistics pipeline to convey direct messages to the Trump administration The choice of Oman also reflects the Iranian plight. Iran needs an intermediary that will not steal the show, but will provide the logistics pipeline to deliver direct messages to the Trump administration. This is an attempt to isolate the dialogue from the noises of the new Middle East. At the end of the day, Oman proves once again that Oman is the country that chooses not to take sides – neither Sunni nor Shia – that becomes the most important asset.

title: סעודיה ואיחוד האמירויות: אינטרסים ויריבות
text: At first glance, relations between Saudi Arabia and the United Arab Emirates appear to be a natural strategic axis: two pragmatic Sunni states, fearing Iranian expansionism and seeking regional stability. But in practice, behind the united front, there has been a quiet rivalry in recent years – one that also has a direct meaning for Israel. a quiet rivalry does not stem from ideology or overt confrontation, but from competition for regional leadership. Saudi Arabia sees Saudi Arabia as the natural leader of the Arab world – a large, relatively conservative country, operating through interstate diplomacy and preserving strong nation-states.

title: סעודיה ואיחוד האמירויות: אינטרסים ויריבות
text: the United Arab Emirates, on the other hand, are a small but ambitious player, operating quickly and in sophistication: investments, controlling ports and trade routes, and building influence through local players even outside state frameworks. This gap has exploded most clearly in Yemen. Saudi Arabia and the United Arab Emirates went out together in a campaign against the Iranian-backed Houthis, but with completely different goals. Saudi Arabia sought first and foremost to protect Saudi Arabia's southern border and keep Yemen united under a central government. the United Arab Emirates focused on the south of Yemen, ports and control of strategic spaces in the Red Sea and the Gulf of Aden.

title: סעודיה ואיחוד האמירויות: אינטרסים ויריבות
text: Over time, the reality prevailed: the central government in Yemen, supported by Saudi Arabia, was greatly weakened. At the same time, the forces of southern Yemen – led by the the United Arab Emirates Southern Transitional Council – have taken over major territories and ports. As far as Riyadh is concerned, this is a strategic failure; as far as Abu Dhabi is concerned, good luck. This gap deepened the distrust between the two allies. China as an additional factor added another significant dimension: China's rise as a major player in the Gulf. China is Riyadh's largest trading partner, primarily in the energy sector, and is an important economic anchor for realizing the 2030 vision.

title: סעודיה ואיחוד האמירויות: אינטרסים ויריבות
text: The Chinese mediation in renewing relations between Riyadh and Iran marked a profound change: for the first time, a non-Western power has shaped a major diplomatic move in the Middle East. But here it is important to distinguish between economics and security. Despite close economic ties with China, Riyadh knows full well that Riyadh's national security rests – and will continue to rely – on an American security umbrella. China does not provide military deterrence, is not committed to protecting oil facilities, and is not prepared to confront Iran on behalf of Riyadh. This is done only by the United States. This is a critical point for Israel as well.

title: סעודיה ואיחוד האמירויות: אינטרסים ויריבות
text: Faced with the Iranian threat – a threat that is no longer theoretical, and that may be decided in the near future through American-Israeli military action – Riyadh understands that Riyadh needs a deep, clear and long-term American commitment. Such a commitment cannot exist in a regional vacuum, and Such a commitment requires Saudi integration into a regional security architecture in which Israel is a central anchor. In this sense, it is precisely Saudi dependency on the United States that is expected to ultimately push for normalization with Israel – even if the road there is complex, fraught with tensions with the United Arab Emirates, and accompanied by attempts at balance with China.

title: סעודיה ואיחוד האמירויות: אינטרסים ויריבות
text: The rivalry between Saudi Arabia and the United Arab Emirates will not disappear, but The rivalry between Saudi Arabia and the UAE will also not deteriorate into an open conflict. For Israel, this is an era of opportunity – provided Israel can read the map correctly and work together with the United States to build a stable regional axis against the Iranian threat. Brig. Gen. text: Like most delegitimization campaigns, even the one waged against the IRGC does not face facts. title: ההפגנה בסכנין: מאבק על החיים עצמם
text: The demonstration and strike that emerged from Sakhnin is not a local protest and is not a response to a single event. The demonstration and strike that emerged from Sakhnin are an expression of a profound and unusual civic moment in Arab society in Israel – a moment of refusal. Refusal to continue living in an ongoing state of emergency, refusal to normalize violence and crime, and refusal to accept promiscuity as a permanent reality. Years of ongoing murder, lack of confidence and repeated disappointments have created deep despair. It is not despair that erupts, but one that gnaws silently: fear that has become routine, murder that has become predictable knowledge, and daily survival that has replaced any discourse on change.

title: ההפגנה בסכנין: מאבק על החיים עצמם
text: Within this reality, many stopped asking how to stop the bleeding, and began to ask only how to get away from ithow to protect themselves as individuals. Precisely on this background, what happened in Sakhnin is not self-evident. The initiative comes not from parties and not from official institutions, but from the street. Shopkeepers, small merchants, people living daily in direct contact with the danger, were the ones who said: Enough. Leadership, at this moment, is not being tested for the ability to calculate steps or to conduct a sophisticated discourse, but for the ability to call for action that is connected to the real plight of citizens. On Thursday, January 22, about 100,000 people came out to demonstrate in Sakhnin.

title: ההפגנה בסכנין: מאבק על החיים עצמם
text: This was preceded by three days of strike, which began with the closure of shops in the city. the closure of shops in the city was not a declarative gesture, but an existential move. Merchants closed Merchants's businesses not out of convenience, but because opening the store became more dangerous than closing the store: sponsorship fees, shootings, threats and destruction are not a minor phenomenon, but a daily reality. There is another painful truth here when crime already dominates the space, it is very difficult to produce resistance from crime. Not because people have given up, but because reality reality itself has reduced choice.

title: ההפגנה בסכנין: מאבק על החיים עצמם
text: So there was a need for an initiative to come from a settlement that saw crime coming in, looked at what had happened to the others, and chose to act before it was too late. the city was a settlement that saw crime coming in, looked at what had happened to the others, and chose to act before it was too late. The demands of the strikers are neither new nor extreme: collecting the illegal weapons, dismantling the criminal organizations, and providing economic solutions that will allow access to legal credit frameworks, rather than being pushed into the black market the struggle is a struggle for life life itself: for the right to leave the house without fear, to work without blackmail, and to live without counting the dead.

title: ההפגנה בסכנין: מאבק על החיים עצמם
text: At the same time, it is impossible to ignore the way the state mediates this reality. Time and again an attempt was made to shift responsibility to Arab society itself – to "culture", "internal violence". It is a worldview that removes responsibility from the state and normalizes the abandonment of human life. The demands of the strikers are neither new nor extreme: collecting illegal weapons, dismantling the criminal organizations, and providing economic solutions that would allow access to legal credit frameworks, rather than being pushed into the black market. In 2025, 252 people were murdered in Arab society, most of 252 people with firearms – a figure that is hard to imagine that would have been accepted with equanimity if it had taken place in another Israeli society.

title: ההפגנה בסכנין: מאבק על החיים עצמם
text: This struggle cannot remain of Arab society alone. This struggle also needs the Jewish public in Israel – with a deep understanding that this is a question of democracy, equality, and shared civic responsibility. A Jewish presence at the demonstration that will take place on January 31 is not a gesture of solidarity alone; A Jewish presence at the demonstration that will take place on January 31 is a clear statement that lifelessness is unacceptable, and that personal security cannot be a privilege of a single group. This is a moment when civil partnership is tested not with words, but by standing together in the public sphere by choice, by values, and by understanding that This struggle is a struggle for the moral character of Israel.

title: כלכלת ישראל בראי 20 השנים האחרונות: לא קריסה, לא נס – דשדוש
text: In recent years, and especially since the outbreak of the war, the public debate surrounding the state of Israel's economy has ranged from two poles. On the one hand, optimistic claims are heard, relying on the relative strength of the shekel, stock market recovery and the durability of the high-tech sector. On the other hand, harsh warnings are raised: low growth, sharp growth in the national debt, declines in credit rating, and the closure of thousands of small businesses. To place these narratives in the correct proportion requires a long-term comparative view, one that neutralizes short-term fluctuations and abnormal world events. We will this end at the performance of the state of Israel's economy over the past 20 years compared to OECD countries, based on IMF data.

title: כלכלת ישראל בראי 20 השנים האחרונות: לא קריסה, לא נס – דשדוש
text: To this end, we will use the mainstream measure accepted among economists: the gross domestic product per capita, correlated by purchasing power (PPP). the mainstream measure accepted among economists: the gross domestic product per capita, correlated by purchasing power (PPP) makes it possible to compare countries while neutralizing gaps in the cost of living, as comparing countries based on GDP at current prices can be misleading: in expensive countries, GDP appears to be higher in monetary terms even if the actual standard of living is not as high as the actual standard of living should be. The adjusted measure by purchasing power is considered the most reliable for assessing living standards and economic progress over time.

title: כלכלת ישראל בראי 20 השנים האחרונות: לא קריסה, לא נס – דשדוש
text: At an absolute glance, the data show a clear picture: the GDP per capita in Israel (adjusted by purchasing power) has risen significantly in the last twenty years at an absolute glance, the data shows a clear picture: the GDP per capita in Israel (adjusted by purchasing power) has risen significantly in the last twenty years. the GDP per capita in Israel (adjusted by purchasing power) more than doubled from $25,069 in 2005 to $55,766 in 2025 – a process that reflected a real improvement in the standard of living of Israeli citizens. However, it is important to place the data in a broader context. The testing period was characterized by accelerated global growth, especially in developed countries, but also in many emerging markets.

title: כלכלת ישראל בראי 20 השנים האחרונות: לא קריסה, לא נס – דשדוש
text: In this sense, the impressive increase in GDP per capita in Israel is not a unique phenomenon, but part of a broad global trend – a fact that reinforces the need to examine Israel's relative performance compared to other countries. However, when comparing Israel to the other OECD countries (a group of 38 developed countries with a relatively high standard of living), a less dramatic picture emerges (see Figure 1). Israel is consistently placed in the third quarter of the table, far from the top. The Israeli shuffle feels like a miss, and illustrates the gap between potential and performance. A country with a world-leading high-tech sector, a high concentration of human capital and technological innovation, could expect higher per capita GDP and sharper ranking progress.

title: כלכלת ישראל בראי 20 השנים האחרונות: לא קריסה, לא נס – דשדוש
text: In practice, we are witnessing a growing gap between Israel and the richest group of countries. In order to enter the Ten Rich Countries club in the OECD ranking in 2025, GDP per capita must be about $75,000, $19,000 more than in Israel. In other words: Israel ran ahead, but others did too. Some – faster. Is the shuffling in Israel's relative ranking a decree of fate? Hell, no. In the past 20 years many countries have been able to significantly accelerate GDP growth per capita through courageous economic policies, which included deep structural reforms and extensive investments in infrastructure and human capital. Figure 2 shows the growth rates of OECD countries that were ranked 22nd to 30th in 2005 – that is, countries whose initial status was very similar to that of Israel.

title: כלכלת ישראל בראי 20 השנים האחרונות: לא קריסה, לא נס – דשדוש
text: The comparison highlights a clear gap: While most countries in the richest group of countries recorded faster GDP growth per capita, Israel displayed a relatively moderate cumulative growth rate, of only about 122 percent. In fact, only Greece and Portugal, affected by the European sovereign debt crisis, showed weaker performance. The significance of a clear gap is not merely theoretical. Had Israel grown in the last 20 years at the average rate of the sample countries not affected by the European crisis – about 153 percent – Israel's per capita GDP would have reached about $63,500. Such a gap is approximately equivalent to an annual increase of over 81,400 shekels per average household.

title: כלכלת ישראל בראי 20 השנים האחרונות: לא קריסה, לא נס – דשדוש
text: In this sense, Israel's's relative shuffling is not the result of inevitable external conditions, but of policy choices that have slowed Israel's's rate of convergence into the richest of countries. The bottom line: Israel's's economy over the past 20 years is not a story of failure, but Israel's economy over the past 20 years is also not a continuing miracle. The standard of living has risen, Israel's economy has expanded, and Israel's clearly belongs to the 50 richest countries in the world. However, the international comparison indicates only a partial utilization of the economic potential: while many countries have managed to narrow gaps and accelerate many countries's rate of convergence to the global economic leadership, Israel's remains stuck in Israel's's relative position.

title: כלכלת ישראל בראי 20 השנים האחרונות: לא קריסה, לא נס – דשדוש
text: Despite a challenging security reality, through proper policy it was possible to move Israel's's economy forward. Unfortunately, the budget that the government is expected to bring to approval of the Knesset does not herald a fundamental change in the future growth rate of Israel's economy. Without structural reforms, a deep and sustained investment in human capital in all sectors and a change in priorities, Israel's relative shuffling could become routine. This is especially against the background of erosion in long-term growth forecasts, partly due to the impact on the productive workforce, both as a result of the war and due to increasing immigration abroad.

title: כלכלת ישראל בראי 20 השנים האחרונות: לא קריסה, לא נס – דשדוש
text: In such a reality, maintaining the status quo is a choice that would mean gradually giving up the place that Israel's could occupy at the top of the global economy. Prof. Esteban Clore is a professor of political economics at the Hebrew University and a senior researcher at INSS.

title: דמשק והכורדים: כרוניקה של הסכמים כושלים
text: Since the rise of Syrian President Ahmad al-Sharah to power, several agreements have been signed between the Syrian regime and the SDF, the Syrian Democratic Forces, a body that mainly includes Kurdish fighters. The most well-known and discussed agreement, which both sides tried to hold on to for the past year, was the March 10, 2025 agreement. Syrian President Ahmad al-Sharah met with the commander of the SDF, the Syrian Democratic Forces, a body that mainly includes Kurdish fighters from Talum Abdi, and came to the understanding that the Kurdish forces would be integrated into the new state institutions. That didn't happen.

title: דמשק והכורדים: כרוניקה של הסכמים כושלים
text: Conflicts between the Kurds and forces affiliated with the administration continued intermittently throughout the year, with both sides constantly making statements about attempts to return to the negotiating table and promoting the implementation of the March 10, 2025 agreement. Tension between the two sides began with clashes in the neighborhoods of Sheikh Maksud and al-Asharfia in the Aleppo province of Syria, expanded to other provinces, A-Raqqa and Deir ez-Zor, and deteriorated to the current situation. The Kurds were pushed further and further, and the territories under The Kurds's control were reduced.

title: הדייט הכי טוב שלכם לא צריך להיות בבית קפה - אלא בסופר
text: A few days ago, a friend called me to develop a dating app for the religious and ultra-Orthodox public. a friend's question was seemingly simple, but fascinating: how can one increase the chance of a date leading to a wedding? Her question is a question that in the secular world is not asked in the same way - where the question is how to reach a long-term relationship. But beyond the linguistic difference, there is a deep behavioral puzzle here: what actually happens on a typical date, and what do we really learn from a typical date about the person in front of us? Try to think of a classic date: two people sitting across from each other, having a drink, and having a conversation.

title: הדייט הכי טוב שלכם לא צריך להיות בבית קפה - אלא בסופר
text: Sounds reasonable, doesn't a classic date: two people sitting across from each other, having a drink, and having a conversation? The problem is that a conversation is based on questions and answers: "Where did you study?", "How many brothers and sisters do you have?", "What do you do in life?". These are questions that don't really reveal who the person in front of us is. We get data, not understanding. It's like trying to understand the taste of a dish in a restaurant just by the list of ingredients on the menu - you can tell there's tomato and basil in there, but is a dish in a restaurant really delicious? How does all this fit together? The ingredients don't tell us the full story.

title: הדייט הכי טוב שלכם לא צריך להיות בבית קפה - אלא בסופר
text: The same goes for dating: we meet, exchange biographical information, and then we have to decide - to continue or stop. And this decision is based on the little real information we've been given about the person in front of us. A few years ago, we conducted an experiment that examined exactly this point. Instead of organizing "ordinary" virtual dating, we asked couples to tour a virtual museum together and talk about what couples seewhat a virtual museum makes couples feel, think, what associations cost couples. The difference was dramatic: when people experience something together, people learn about each other in a completely different way. You see how the other person thinks, what excites the other person, what the other person's values world is.

title: הדייט הכי טוב שלכם לא צריך להיות בבית קפה - אלא בסופר
text: It's not information you get in answer to one question or another - it's something you experience. You hear the other person's spontaneous reactions, what makes the other person laugh, what makes the other person angry. And the data confirmed my intuition: participants who were on a museum date wanted much more to meet again than those who were on a "normal" date. Not just a little – the difference was significant and obvious. So what did I offer the friend who called me? Two options. First, if you stay in the digital world - build shared experiences that allow people to understand how the other person thinks and feels, and not just find out what the other person are answering questions about.

title: הדייט הכי טוב שלכם לא צריך להיות בבית קפה - אלא בסופר
text: Something similar to our virtual museum, which will create opportunities for real and unprepared responses. And the other, in my more interesting view: going out together to make arrangements. Shopping at the supermarket. A hardware store to buy light bulbs or batteries. Fold laundry. Arrange a cabinet. Return something to the store. For what? Because when you do something real with someone, you really see them. Do you see how he handles small decisions (which cheese to buy? Is it worth buying on sale?), how does he react to light stress (doesn't have the product we wanted), how does he treat other people (copier, seller), what is his sense of humor when things don't go smoothly.

title: הדייט הכי טוב שלכם לא צריך להיות בבית קפה - אלא בסופר
text: And yes, there is also a small advantage here – you are doing what you should have done anyway, just using that time to get to know each other too. Instead of spending two hours on a date and then another hour shopping, you combine both. Evolution has taught us to this ability who is facing us through what he is doing, not through his resume. For thousands of years, this ability has been critical to survivaland this ability still puts our brains no good at understanding people based on what people say about people. We all know how to present a flattering version on a date: picking the good stories, blurring sharp corners, looking our best.

title: הדייט הכי טוב שלכם לא צריך להיות בבית קפה - אלא בסופר
text: But we are excellent at reading behavior – evolution has taught us to understand who is standing in front of us through what he is doing, not through his resume. For thousands of years, this ability has been critical to survivaland this ability is still there, if we just allow this ability to take action. So the next time you're planning a date, consider: Instead of a coffee shop, maybe a writer? Instead of a movie in the cinema, maybe IKEA? You will not only get to know the other person better – you will also find out much faster if it really fits. And if it doesn't fit, you've saved yourself valuable time. And if any of you are trying this method - I would be very happy to hear how this method worked. Maybe we'll learn something new together.

For Moscow, the crisis on the Greenland issue and the Greenland issue's future is a kind of geopolitical gift, allowing President Putin to try and continue President Putin's efforts to deepen the disagreements between the U.S. and Europe, and to sabotage the unity of the NATO alliance. This happens in difficult times for President Putin: the impressive U.S.

title: המלכודת הרוסית: בין תמיכה בטראמפ לבוז לאירופה

title: המלכודת הרוסית: בין תמיכה בטראמפ לבוז לאירופה
text: Lavrov sent a sting to the Europeans, of course, when Lavrov said that the Europeans support the right of Greenlanders to decide Greenlanders's fate but refuse to recognize the right of the Donbass people in eastern Ukraine or the Crimea to do so as well. Despite the enormous irony, Moscow enjoys introducing the United States, even that of Trump, as a dour power that does not respect the sovereignty of the United States's closest allies, thereby trying to sow fear and suspicion among other European countries towards the United States net gain for Moscow. Putin Putin himself referred to the U.S. ambitions in Greenland ten months ago, as part of Putin himself's speech at the Arctic Forum in Murmansk in northwestern Russia.

title: המלכודת הרוסית: בין תמיכה בטראמפ לבוז לאירופה
text: Putin himself said at the time in a speech, among other things, that Trump's plans for Greenland are "a topic that concerns two specific countries and has nothing to do with us. It would be a profound mistake not to take Trump's words seriously. the United States has had plans regarding Greenland since the 1860s. the United States has serious plans for Greenland.

title: המלכודת הרוסית: בין תמיכה בטראמפ לבוז לאירופה
text: These programs have long historical roots, as Putin have just pointed out, and it is clear that it will continue to consistently advance its geostrategic, military-political and economic interests in the Arctic." But then, Putin added the issue of NATO, saying: "Of course, Russia are concerned about the fact that NATO countries as a whole are increasingly targeting the far north as a springboard for possible conflicts and are practicing using forces under these conditions, including with the forces of NATO countries as a whole's new recruits - Finland and Sweden, with whom, by the way, until recently Russia had no problems at all." This is an excellent illustration of the Russian approach, on the one hand claiming that Russia has no interest in Greenland, but at the same time warned that Trump desires Greenland very much, and that NATO is also leveraging NATO's presence in favor of promoting possible positions with Russia.

title: המלכודת הרוסית: בין תמיכה בטראמפ לבוז לאירופה
text: On the face of it, there is a contradiction: the Russians are not supposed to be happy to see American ownership of Greenland and Greenland's transformation into one huge military base in an area of critical importance to Russia. So why do the Russians express such understanding and openness to Trump's moves there? title: אין ברירה: צריך לדבר על אובדנות
text: Since the beginning of the war, every few weeks there is a news release that turns the stomach: a fighter who has returned from the front and died following a loss. Unfortunately, science and trends are clear – suicides are going to prevail. Not because people have become weaker, but because the mental load in this country crosses human boundaries. Suicide is not the problem – Suicide is the extreme symptom of the problem. Suicide is the end of a long series of emotional challenges: loneliness, lack of response, lack of meaning, uncontrollable emotional inundation, lack of emotional clarity, burnout, unemployment, unprocessed trauma. When a person reaches the end of this sequence, it is clear that this is not a point event. It's a system failure.

title: אין ברירה: צריך לדבר על אובדנות
text: Dealing with suicide is being trapped inside your head, being trapped in a continuous toxic conversation. It's being alone with disturbing repetitive thoughts, no time-outs, no stop button. Those who have experienced suicide know: suicide is not necessarily a one-time event that reaches a dramatic peak, suicide is more a long and slow process of daily asphyxiation. When we don't talk about suicide, we actually leave people stuck alone in people's own heads, without equipping people with words and sentences to stop the toxic conversation. We tell people that we have to deal quietly, alone, without disturbing us, without asking us for help. The logic behind this unwritten agreement is understandable: in order not to encourage suicide, we will not talk about suicide.

title: אין ברירה: צריך לדבר על אובדנות
text: But that logic no longer works. The reality on the ground is that the phenomenon is only growing. It's time to change the perception. A public discourse about suicide is like a lifeline. If we talk about suicide, about how you get out of this toxic conversation in your head, about ways, about tools, about possibilities – whoever needs to hear may hear. Not all of them. Not always. Not right away. But the cliché says that enough is enough. One person is enough to realize that One person is not unusual, that One person is not broken, that there are other options besides closing matters. The first will bring the second with it, and so over time, a cultural infrastructure of resilience will be created. The situation today is such that there are not enough torturers.

title: בין 7 באוקטובר לאנטישמיות: למה אנחנו תמיד מחפשים אשמים
text: Searching for guilty is one of the things we're best at. When this bad thing happens, our first instinct is to try to understand why this bad thing happened, and the second instinct is to find a reasonand preferably one that has nothing to do with us in any way. I mean, even if there's guilt, guilt's not ours. We are able to search and search until found guilty. The third thing that drives us in the quest for guilt is the desire to find a simple story: we want a story about good and bad where we are good. Someone else is bad, and the story is a simple one. Why is it important for us to have a simple story? Because we want to have some sort of rule of thumb, to have an understanding of how to prevent this bad thing from happening again.

title: בין 7 באוקטובר לאנטישמיות: למה אנחנו תמיד מחפשים אשמים
text: Another reason for our search for culprits is the desire to find a disproportionate reason for the magnitude of the disaster. If something big happened, we're looking for a huge reason. If something small happened, we'd settle for a little reason. By the way, when something good happens, we're not necessarily looking for the person who caused it. title: זה הזמן להכיר במשבר החינוכי - ולפתור אותו
text: We have all felt in recent years the lack of teachers - the lack of teachers is not a point systemic problem, but a present depth crisis, which will worsen. Like the rest of the Western countries, Israel is faced with the collapse of teaching power, but unlike the spin that teachers' organizations spread, the reasons for the collapse of teaching power are not just dependent on teachers' salaries. In a world of dissolving community identities, undermining traditional powers, and weakening public spacethe educator figure loses spacethe educator figure's status, depletes spacethe educator figure's meaning, and thereby is fatally harmed. In some ways, Israel has no absolute shortage of teachers. There are quite a few teachers on the national level.

title: זה הזמן להכיר במשבר החינוכי - ולפתור אותו
text: But the more established a sector or community, the more a sector or community deplete local educational forces and entrust a sector or community's children to foreign perceptions of discourse at home and in the community. The price is, for example, a non-Jewish teacher delivering content for Rosh Hashanah or for Memorial Day. The local shortage also results in the fact that even if the teacher fits in with the teacher's educational outlook, the teacher does not always know the material being studied – and this is how the indexes of grades are going down in recent years. This generation is no less good than This generation's predecessor, but the problem starts from above.

title: זה הזמן להכיר במשבר החינוכי - ולפתור אותו
text: If we want to hold the traditional Jewish concept of education, a concept of "the people of the book" that saw the educator as a role model and a full partner community, we must return education to the top of the national priorities and restore responsibility for education to the community and the locality. There is no escape from deep, ongoing and costly investment, but no less than a perceptual shift. No longer a disregard for the profession of education, but a collective responsibility of all of us to the education system and an understanding that education is a community mission. We need to get under the education stretchers before it's too late, and institutionalize young people's mission in local educators' communities. Imagine a 25-year-old.

title: זה הזמן להכיר במשבר החינוכי - ולפתור אותו
text: After military service, a bachelor's degree, maybe a preparatory study, a world trip, and occasional jobs. Just before a 25-year-old find a relationship and a permanent professional horizon, a 25-year-old are given an opportunity to give back to the community by joining a young mission in a community of urban educators. The municipality takes care of fully subsidized housing for a specified period of time, proper training within the educational institution and integration into a local school. The deal is simple: a first year of training for a teaching certificate funded by the Ministry of Education and within the school where a 25-year-old will be taught later, followed by four years of full educational work at the school where a 25-year-old experimented.

title: זה הזמן להכיר במשבר החינוכי - ולפתור אותו
text: At the end of five years, the mission can be completed, and if the teacher wants to stay - the municipality and the school decide whether to offer the teacher an extension of the mission period. Those who are selected and wish to continue, are also awarded a postgraduate scholarship in any field they wish. After a decade the educator finishes, separates from the community of educators and chooses the educator's path, with an advanced degree and a stable and worthy salary, distinguished educational experience, community and a cohesive professional identity. the educator can choose to stay in education, or choose another professional path, as the educator is already an established and meaningful person who has contributed and contributed.

title: זה הזמן להכיר במשבר החינוכי - ולפתור אותו
text: The state and municipality are divided in housing ownership, the Ministry of Education carries training and scholarships, and the local authority becomes an active educational player and not just a technical operator of schools. This model is not as revolutionary as This model seems. It mentions the perception of education in Jewish communities around the world, where educators are absorbed into a fixed period, are fully enveloped and in return assimilated into community life.

title: זה הזמן להכיר במשבר החינוכי - ולפתור אותו
text: In an age of detachment, loneliness, and the erosion of commitment, connection, connection, and reciprocity are the most important currency. This is how motivated educators who are connected to the community, speak the community's values, live within the community, and become significant figures in the world of children and youth. We will gain living, relevant, educational institutions that restore the educator's natural place as a pillar of community. The teachers' crisis is a wake-up call. title: כשהמלחמה מגיעה עם הלוחמים הביתה: האתגר הערכי של המערכת
text: An iron sword war is not just a large-scale military campaign – An iron sword war is a formative event that shakes Israeli society and tests Israeli society's moral, moral, and institutional systems. Alongside the heroism, volunteerism and willingness to shoulder the burden, it becomes clear that there is a painful gap between the reality experienced by the fighters and the rules and definitions by which the state recognizes the price the fighters are paying. the state's method of recognizing IDF casualties and the fallen in the state's systems rests on clear legal and administrative criteria, which are intended to ensure uniformity, equality and certainty.

title: כשהמלחמה מגיעה עם הלוחמים הביתה: האתגר הערכי של המערכת
text: However, the current war, in the current war's intensity and duration, has created a new reality: prolonged fighting, endless repetition to the battlefield, extreme mental loads, and continued exposure to loss, trauma, and life-threatening danger. a new reality: prolonged fighting, endless repetition to the battlefield, extreme mental loads, and continued exposure to loss, trauma, and life-threatening danger is not always in line with the existing definitions, but a new reality: prolonged fighting, endless repetition to the battlefield, extreme mental loads, and continued exposure to loss, trauma, and life-threatening danger requires a re-examination of the existing definitions.

title: כשהמלחמה מגיעה עם הלוחמים הביתה: האתגר הערכי של המערכת
text: Since the outbreak of war, there has been a significant increase in mental distress among regular and reserve fighters. Many of regular and reserve fighters return to their home after weeks and months of fighting, only to find that the real battle begins there. Mental injuries do not always erupt in a single clear moment or in a defined operational framework. Sometimes Mental injuries develop gradually, after the end of active service, but Mental injuries's roots are deeply rooted in the combat experiences the combat experiences themselves. The case of the late Josh (Joshua) Boone brutally illustrates this gap. Josh was a lone soldier, fighter and commander, who performed over 700 reserve days and served in a series of combat units, including Golani, Paratroopers and Alexandroni.

title: כשהמלחמה מגיעה עם הלוחמים הביתה: האתגר הערכי של המערכת
text: Josh took an active part in combat and operational activity, while at the same time making an outstanding contribution to the community of lone soldiers – training, escorting and strengthening hundreds of young people on hundreds of young people on their way to meaningful service in the IDF's way to meaningful service in the IDF. This is a big news for the Upper Galilee region and the State of Israel in general, but at the same time there is a price in parting from the ancient but so relevant term on the northern border: Tel Hai. "Tel Hai" is not only a term that was embedded in the public memory mainly thanks to Joseph Trumpeldor about a century ago, but a value perception that has lived to this day. The kibbutzim in the Upper Galilee region, historically, did not evacuate The kibbutzim in the Upper Galilee's homes during the war even when there was a severe security escalation, such as the First and Second Lebanon Wars.

text: The "last shock" in Joseph Trumpeldor's words is not just agricultural cultivation but a settlement that clings to the land under all conditions. Processing "The Last Tamer" is done near the place of residence, and is a task that involves the daily physical connection to the place of residence. But not everyone clings to the ground in the same way that Joseph Trumpeldor claimed. In the surrounding communities, as opposed to the north, the prevailing perception is that seven times of security tension, it is better to evacuate to the absorbing settlements until anger passes. This concept seeks to create a separation between civilian life and military life. The need for separation is understandable, when one looks at what happened to many kibbutzim and settlements during the war.

title: בחירות התקומה: המחיר שאנו חייבים לשלם
text: Two years after October 7, it's still hard to pretend. October 7 was not just an intelligence or operational failure, but the collapse of the Israeli illusion: that it is possible to run a divided state, with weak institutions and crumbling civilian trust – and still survive. What exploded October 7 was the idea that decisions could be postponed, accountability obscured, and policy-building on improvisation. It all drains into one moment when the system has ceased to function. The truth that has since been uncovered is staggering: Israel knows how to win wars – but finds it difficult to run a country in a routine fashion, to the point of civil abandonment. This is not a point failure, but an ongoing depth failure.

title: בחירות התקומה: המחיר שאנו חייבים לשלם
text: Israel, in Israel's current institutional structure, finds it difficult to cope with fundamental tasks of governance, planning and service to the citizen. Neither the government, nor the judicial system, nor the elites nor the divided public into tribesnone of them alone are able to lead a revival. The question is no longer who is to blame, but whether someone is willing to pay the price of the amendment. Israeli society has been torn between a liberal concept that seeks to curb power, and a national one that requires broad governance and decision-making ability. These two perceptions were part of Israel's success, but instead of balance, the rift conflict was not born in war. the rift conflict exploded in front of Israel.

These two perceptions were part of Israel's's success, but instead of balance, a conflict was created. Progressive liberalism is cut off against extreme communal nationalism. No "and" connection. A recipe for collision. The protest of 2023, the gaps between center and periphery, the national, ideological, sectarian and religious rifts – all of which have not disappeared. The war only exposed them. Public confidence in the military, government, justice system, and public service has been eroded in a way that cannot be restored by slogans about "unity".

title: בחירות התקומה: המחיר שאנו חייבים לשלם

The Trump administration, which has returned to the White House with a clear grasp of power and a pursuit of historical heritage, is seeking dramatic moves in the regional arena: broad normalization, intense reconstruction in a multi-national framework, and a reduction in friction that will allow for long-term stability. Israel's has opened a window of opportunity – but a clear limitation has also been placed: there is no longer room for escape from political and civil responsibility. Towards the 2026 election, the political system will be put to the test. A politics of hate and "us or them" will deepen the rift.

title: בחירות התקומה: המחיר שאנו חייבים לשלם
text: But even a purely technical administration, without a clear vision and without deep reforms, will not suffice. Israel needs no more talk of reconciliation, but rather a policy of reconciliation: reform of government and public service, the reorganization among the authorities, the restoration of civilian infrastructure and civic education that strengthens cohesion. The 2026 election will be a fate election. Between the politics of revenge and the politics of responsibility. Between decomposition and remodeling. If we continue to act as if everything is temporary, everything will collapse. If we pay the price of responsibility, we might be able to say that October 7 was not only a day of destruction, but also a starting point for the revival. Prof.

title: עם האצבע על ההדק
text: Hamas''s murderous attack on October 7, 2023 will be remembered as one of the greatest traumas in Israel's's history, one that constitutes a strategic turning point in the history of a people, often along with the declaration of "never again". When European countries, for example, declared war on each other in 1914, confident of European countries's armies' ability to make a swift decision, European countries accepted in return the bloody shuffle of the trench war, in which millions of soldiers died in vain.

title: עם האצבע על ההדק
text: In the early 2000s, the United States was confident that the United States would quickly and easily change the regimes of Iraq and Afghanistan, getting stuck for years in Iraq and Afghanistan, and eventually, after the sacrifice and waste of countless resources, came out without any real achievements. Israel's, on the other hand, believed Israel's could "deter" and "contain" Hamas', and lost about 1,200 of Israel's's civilians and soldiers, who were burned, murdered, raped and languished for months and years in Gaza's tunnels. Some believe that Israel's was even in existential danger due to the collapse of the defensive lines on the first day, especially if Hezbollah had also joined the campaign.

title: עם האצבע על ההדק
text: In all of the above-mentioned cases, the trauma has led to a strategic turning point, sometimes resulting in further disasters – only in the other direction, a phenomenon known in the study as overcompensation. Two examples will suffice: the trauma of the European powers from World War I led to a "no more war" approach, and from there to appeasement that allowed Hitler to conquer Europe in World War II. Similarly, the trauma of Iraq and Afghanistan has so permeated the American public that various governments have avoided even the necessary interventions in foreign countries and entered into a form of strategic paralysis. Israel, for Israel's part, should be careful not to fall into the same trap of "overcompensation" after October 7.

title: עם האצבע על ההדק
text: The trauma of October 7 taught us that an alert cannot be trusted against the murderous intentions of an enemy, and certainly it cannot be assumed that an enemy is "disgusting". Thus, the enemy's capabilities must be destroyed even before the enemy's capabilities develop, as well as maintaining "pressure zones" to protect us from attack. Israel is currently implementing this doctrine in Gaza, Lebanon and to a large extent in Syria as well. These conclusions are not preposterous, and These conclusions make a lot of sense. This is true for opponents such as Hamas, and to some extent Hezbollah and Iran, because the destruction of Israel is in a very high place, if not the only one, in opponents such as Hamas, and to some extent Hezbollah and Iran's priorities.

title: עם האצבע על ההדק
text: When the opponent prioritizes murder of Israelis over almost any other interest, it is difficult to predict when an opportunity will identify an attack, and it is also difficult to deter an attack by charging a high price – the attack on Israel's is simply too important to the opponent. For this reason, I have read during the war with Hamas to prioritise the replacement of this regime in Gaza over any other consideration, and this is also supposed to be in other parts of the Iranian axis, within Israel's's limitations and capabilities (Hezbollah and Iran Israel's cannot completely eradicate, but Hezbollah and Iran's capabilities can be curtailed and weakened, in the hope that internal rivals will at some point complete the work).

title: עם האצבע על ההדק

Although Turkey is certainly Israel's's strongest and most dangerous adversary in the region these days, it would be a mistake to throw Israel's's way of dealing with Iran at Turkey. The two opponents are different, and the ways of dealing with The two opponents should be different accordingly. In the face of other adversaries, a policy must be conducted that involves military threat, capacity building, diplomacy, and alliances, in order to save precious resources and isolate Israel's adversaries instead of being isolated. This is, to a large extent, the real lesson to be learned from the events of October 7. Prof. Danny Orbach is a military historian from the Hebrew University of Jerusalem.

In political and military theory, this is a fundamental condition – stability is not a natural state but a product of constant adjustment to a changing reality. Strategy is always temporary, conditioned and adaptable, because the world in which Strategy operates is in constant motion. Interests change, coalitions fall apart and are rebuilt, and yesterday's enemy may have the same interests and vice versa. title: ריבונות למראית עין
text: On May 11, 2022, the political system in Israel entered a state of nerve-racking waiting. It was not a Knesset plenum discussion or a cabinet meeting that determined the future of Israel, but rather a meeting of the Shura Council of the Islamic Movement. While the Knesset dispersal law was on the table and the coalition was leaning on chicken pastures, Israel was awaiting the departure of clerics in a closed room to know whether the IRGC party would continue to freeze the IRGC party's membership or bring about the downfall of the government.

title: ריבונות למראית עין
text: These moments revealed a deep crack in Israeli democracy: a situation in which fateful national decisions that shape the identity of government and state security are not accepted in official government institutions, but rather in internal forums that are not elected by the general public and are not committed to the general public. With all the known differences, what we saw then with the Shura Council of the Islamic Movement, we saw again this week with the Council of Torah Sages: Israel stopped and waited for the decision of a body that no one had chosen, which is not subject to any voter, and whose decisions effectively determine the stability of the coalition and the future of the government.

title: ריבונות למראית עין
text: In both cases the issue discussed among those unelected bodies was the borders of the sovereignty of Israel. In one case, the government's's intention to enforce the law on the Temple Mount was challenged, and in the present case, the government's's authority to order conscription for all was undermined. The Knesset of Israel, which is supposed to ensure that fateful decisions are accepted by elected officials responsible to elected officials responsible to their constituents's constituents, has been trapped among the chairs of internal power systems operating outside of the public sphere. True, on the surface the system functions as a series: citizens vote, parties receive mandates, and coalitions are formed.

title: ריבונות למראית עין
text: But democracy is tested on how democracy actually functions, and democracy does not amount to going to the polls every few years. The fate of Israel should be determined in institutions that represent the public in all the public in all its shades and transparent to its criticism's shades and transparent to the public in all its shades and transparent to its criticism's criticism. When closed councils, which do not bear responsibility for all citizens, are the ones that decide the boundaries of sovereignty of state institutions within Israel, we witness the depletion of sovereignty. This is true for the Supreme Court, but also for the Shura Council and the Council of Torah Sages, and it doesn't matter how close you feel to one at the expense of the other.

title: ריבונות למראית עין
text: The fact that these bodies can create political crises, break up coalitions and impose agendas is not just political discomfort. The fact that these bodies can create political crises, break up coalitions and impose agendas is a symptom of deep structural failure. It is important to clarify: this is not an intention to discredit certain communities or incite animosity. The criticism is not directed at their identity, but towards the mechanism by which the national decision-making centers pass into the hands of bodies that are not committed to broad representation, transparency, or democratic control. It is impossible to stop judicial activism in order to restore Israeli democracy.

title: ריבונות למראית עין
text: When a small group, chosen by a handful of people or by internal leadership, decides the fate of millions of citizens, a small group, chosen by a handful of people or by internal leadership is no less a serious threat. Will we continue to talk about the will of the people while decisions of existential weight are made outside the elected institutions? Or will we take responsibility and amend the rules so that the power is truly restored to the right-of-choice people? title: המשטר האיראני עומד בפני נפילה?
text: The current wave of protests in Iran is reposing the question of the stability of the Islamic regime, but this time under severe and fundamentally different conditions than those that characterized the previous waves of protest. A rare combination of economic collapse, deep infrastructural crises, public consciousness change, and increasing international pressure create a volatile reality in which the very existence of the Islamic regime is not as self-evident as the very existence of the regime once was. The basis of The current wave of protests in Iran is primarily existential-economic. The Iranian rial is at a historic low, losing The Iranian rial's value at an accelerated pace and erasing middle-class savings.

title: המשטר האיראני עומד בפני נפילה?
text: Unlike the past, where the Islamic regime has managed to contain shocks through subsidies or emergency measures, today the Islamic regime's economic capacity is very limited. The collapse of The Iranian rial directly translates into a sharp price rise, a lack of basic goods, and a sense of a complete loss of state control over the economy. This is further compounded by the water crisis and the electricity crisis, which have transformed from regional problems into a total national crisis. Severe water shortages affect large cities, agriculture, and industry, and frequent power outages also undermine the routine of living in urban centers.

title: לוין ורוטמן אימצו את תפיסתו הפרשנית של ברק

Reuven says to Shimon: Reuven offer to sell Shimon a horse that Reuven own at a certain price. Answer: Shimon agree to buy a horse that I own at a certain price. Supposedly a clear contract. In reality, however, both sides did not mean a horse that I own at all. both sides called between both sides an old machine possessed by Reuven by the affectionate name "Horse". That's how both sides talked, that's how both sides wrote, that's how both sides negotiated. If we stand on the language of the contract alone, we will come to an absurd result: the sale of an animal that was never on the table.
